headline: Halal Certification Disputes Affect 2,400 Non-Muslim Businesses
context: JAKIM's expanded halal certification requirements introduced in 2024 have affected 2,400 non-Muslim-owned food businesses, with 340 losing certification due to supply chain audit failures.

cards.big: 340 non-Muslim food businesses lost halal certification in 2024.
cards.sub: New supply chain requirements mean a single non-halal ingredient supplier can invalidate an entire business's certification.

cards.big: Halal certification compliance costs rose 45% under the 2024 framework.
cards.sub: Small businesses report spending RM28,000-65,000 annually. 89% of affected businesses are SMEs.

cards.big: Malaysia's halal export market is worth RM48B annually.
cards.sub: Stricter standards serve export credibility but impose domestic costs on businesses serving local markets.

cards.big: Non-Muslim business owners have no appeal mechanism within JAKIM.
cards.sub: Appeals go to a JAKIM internal panel. No independent adjudication body exists.

cards.big: When non-Muslim businesses cannot appeal halal decisions to an independent body, the system lacks basic procedural fairness.
Halal standards are legitimate. But procedural fairness — independent appeals, proportionate costs, transition support — is equally legitimate. Both can coexist.
